question.text: Scenario: Priya Sharma, aged 42, is a salaried private sector employee. Her income details for FY 2025-26 are as follows: - Basic Salary: Rs. 18,00,000 per annum - Dearness Allowance (part of retirement benefits): Rs. 3,60,000 per annum - HRA received: Rs. 7,20,000 per annum (she pays rent of Rs. 40,000 per month in Mumbai) - Employer's contribution to EPF: 12% of (Basic + DA) = Rs. 2,59,200 - Employer's contribution to NPS: 12% of (Basic + DA) = Rs. 2,59,200 - Priya's own contribution to EPF: Rs. 2,59,200 - Priya's own contribution to NPS under 80CCD(1B): Rs. 50,000 - Priya's PPF investment: Rs. 1,50,000 - Life insurance premium: Rs. 80,000 - Health insurance premium: Rs. 28,000 (self and family, all below 60) + Rs. 35,000 (parents, both senior citizens) - Interest on education loan for her son: Rs. 1,20,000 (3rd year of repayment) - Interest on self-occupied house property loan: Rs. 3,50,000 - Short-term capital gains (STT paid, listed equity): Rs. 2,50,000 - Long-term capital gains (STT paid, equity MF): Rs. 3,00,000 - Dividend income from Indian companies: Rs. 80,000 - Savings account interest: Rs. 12,000 Priya seeks advice from her investment adviser on whether to opt for old or new tax regime. Question 4 of 5: Under the new tax regime (section 115BAC) for FY 2025-26, what is the total amount of deductions/exemptions available to Priya from her salary income and under Chapter VI-A?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Under the new tax regime (section 115BAC) for FY 2025-26, very few deductions and exemptions are allowed. For a salaried employee, the permissible deductions are:
(1) Standard deduction from salary: Rs. 75,000 (enhanced from Rs. 50,000 under the new regime from FY 2024-25 onwards).
(2) Section 80CCD(2) — Employer's NPS contribution: For private sector employees under the new regime, the deduction limit is 14% of salary (Basic + DA). 14% of Rs. 21,60,000 = Rs. 3,02,400. However, the actual employer contribution is only Rs. 2,59,200 (12% of salary). The deduction is limited to the ACTUAL contribution = Rs. 2,59,200.
(3) NOT available under new regime: HRA exemption (section 10(13A)), Section 80C, Section 80CCD(1B), Section 80D, Section 80E, Section 80TTA, and interest deduction on self-occupied house property under section 24(b).
Total deductions = Rs. 75,000 + Rs. 2,59,200 = Rs. 3,34,200.
**The Trap:** Rs. 75,000 ignores the 80CCD(2) deduction which IS available under the new regime — this is a commonly missed benefit. Rs. 3,77,400 uses 14% of salary (Rs. 3,02,400) as the 80CCD(2) deduction instead of the actual contribution of Rs. 2,59,200 — the deduction is limited to the lower of the percentage cap or the actual contribution. Rs. 6,81,000 incorrectly includes HRA exemption and 80CCD(1B), which are NOT available under the new regime.
**Cross-Exam Context:** The new regime's 80CCD(2) limit for private sector was increased from 10% to 14% of salary from FY 2024-25 onwards — this is a frequently tested update. Remember: 80CCD(2) is one of the very few Chapter VI-A deductions retained in the new regime because it represents employer generosity, not employee tax planning.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Priya's total deductions collapse from Rs. 11,20,000 in the old regime (HRA Rs. 2,64,000 + Std ded Rs. 50,000 + HP interest Rs. 2,00,000 + Chapter VI-A Rs. 6,06,000) to just Rs. 3,34,200 in the new regime — a reduction of Rs. 7,85,800. Whether this gap is compensated by more favorable slab rates in the new regime determines the optimal choice.
